videos.title: Germany's DAX index to remain 'pretty flat' for the rest of the year, Goldman Sachs says
videos.text: I think the the predicament that the economy is facing at the moment is really down to a number of factors which Go including the online energy policies towards Russia over the previous 20 years exactly and so you know we found ourselves in a situation where Energy prices went up a lot uh there's a strategic disadvantage in Germany and indeed across Europe but particularly in Germany in terms of the cost of manufacturing manufacturing is weak anyway because of the effects of rising interest rates and the cost of capital and the weakness in China so it's just got a lot of exposure to that one of the interesting things about the conference though is that it's looking at Mid and small size companies in particular and there's a lot of vibrant activity taking place in terms of newer companies coming through a lot of them are not represented in the main index but there there are a lot of opportunities just on that point you just raised it the the these middle German companies I have often been I was going to say subservient that's not the right point a suppliers into the the beer moths into Siemens into the false Bargains of this world are we saying actually that the the attitude and the direction of travel for a lot of these these middle German companies is actually in a New Direction rather than actually their traditional role of being a supplier to the enormous German companies I think there is some of that certainly I mean many not all of the smaller companies are also looking more globally they're more Tech orientated and those they see themselves as global companies that just happen to be in Germany and that's true for a lot of European companies that are headquartered in a particular country but don't really see their customer base or indeed their their their their their future purely in that in that country and we should recognize that even the Dax leading large companies um you know have significant exposure to the global economy uh around 20 or so of their exposure is to Asia itself that's been a little bit weak recently but they're not full really reflective of the weakness in the German economy itself and that's one of the reasons why earnings have held up better in Germany than many people thought and indeed the stock market has done reasonably well this year despite the economic challenges there is a mini chip felt also unfolding right in Germany and that's key and the United States has one so ecosystem matters how does that hold up though when we talk about monetary policy I mean we just heard from de gallo this morning that look we've got to get back to the two percent Mark before we start shuffling that rate across Europe the Hawks might be welcoming that news from Germany there's no shortage of hawks in Germany but can the fiscal policy compensate for the monetary policy when it comes to some of these startup growth areas of the economy well I think the the you know one of the key differences at least for financial markets is the shift in the cost of capital and it's been really meaningful and we shouldn't forget that in Europe in particular you know rates were going from a minus number for for several years to a positive number and there's no guarantee that they haven't got further to to go and bear in mind before the financial crisis German 10-year Bundy odds were about four percent they've still got some way to get to that if indeed to go back to that level there is an easing of fiscal policy and there's more cause to do more on that side but the effect I think on on funding of businesses through the impact of financial conditions and the cost of capital is still pretty material that's true of course for companies everywhere but fiscal policy does play an important part and particularly so in manufacturing because so many companies are looking at the more constructive or Direct effects of fiscal policy in places like the us through things like the inflation reduction act can I bring up the Dax a lot of the action really at the the front end of the curve for this this year you saw the starter 23 the Dax are bounced in fact we're still high to date choppy action really sideways since about April what's the the rest of the the index look like for this this year I think pretty flat still um I mean more of the same what we have underlying the market not just in Germany but across Europe is basically zero profit growth this year what a disaster given the weakness in the economy but certainly nothing very exciting by the way that's true in the US as well next year we're looking at about five percent but when you consider where interest rates have gone you do have a risk-free alternative which is pretty attractive um and uh you know I think that with interest rates staying higher for longer that's been our view for some time it's still going to be a while before we see any strong directional rise I think in in the market in Germany across Europe or indeed even globally we think most equities are stuck in a relatively flat range for a period of time at least until really interest rates start to come down

videos.title: Germany unveils $65bn inflation-relief plan
videos.text: the huge rises in wholesale gas prices have set off an inflationary shockwave through europe russia's announcement on friday that its nordstrom one pipeline would remain shut down is set to send energy prices spiking yet again this week you will never walk alone germany's chancellor is trying to ease fears among households and businesses with a new package of aid we are talking about 65 billion euros when you add everything together and we are talking about 95 billion if you include the first two relief packages that's a lot we are doing and it's necessary germany's finance minister apparently is now persuaded of the need for a windfall tax on energy suppliers worth in the double-digit billions he says the german government is saying the country can last the winter having built up gas reserves to 85 percent of capacity but energy stockpiling by germany and other european countries has helped send prices skyward along with fears for millions facing fuel poverty it was announced just this morning that italy spent 100 billion euros on on gas and energy and is one of the most exposed to russian imports and to put that in context normally the costs are around 12 billion euros for a country like that so this is very expensive and what we're seeing now is those costs starting to spill over the question for european leaders is to what extent this economic pressure could splinter the united front in supporting ukraine the czech prime minister says saturday's street protests were organized by radical russia sympathizing elements nonetheless police said 70 000 people took part this winter russia is preparing for a decisive energy strike on all europeans and the key answers to this should be two things first our unity unity in defense against the terrorist state and second increasing our own pressure on russia olaf schultz's second set piece event of the day designed to support that message welcoming ukraine's prime minister with a military guard of honour dennis shmihal is seeking more military aid from berlin and is offering to send surplus ukrainian electricity the other way harry fawcett al jazeera

videos.title: Germany Sets Aside Billions for LNG Purchases
videos.text: the German government has set aside billions of Euros for liquefied natural gas purchases in an effort to save off an energy crisis this winter for more we're joined by energy and climate change reporter Vanessa dezem Vanessa good morning to you so the German government seems to be in a rush then to get more LNG supplies this has been work in progress for many months now what difference will it make for the winter that they're setting aside this money it is it is very important so LNG has been key to Germany not only to Germany but also for Europe uh and we are seeing record LNG ports in EU month after month so it has been very very important if you were feeling up to fill up storage sites and that's key also to get prepared for the winter we are ahead we are just weeks ahead from the heating season when demand starts to increase and now Germany has its uh storage facilities 90 full and thanks in part to lnd Imports uh so yeah German authorities uh say the country is well prepared now to go through the winter even if Russia cuts uh all cuts off everything also applies to to the region and now Germany yeah needs to keep bringing LNG and uh and yeah it's turning a lot of money on that it's very important for the next month Vanessa one of the questions I wanted to clarify is that Germany doesn't have the infrastructure to import LNG in this kind of scale so what are the challenges of bringing the fuel in that's correct so Germany has new energy terminals So Far So before the crisis the country was reluctant and to tie itself to uh with fossil fuels and let's remember LNG is a polluting source of energy so but now it seems to be the only solution in the short term so Germany is now investing in several LNG terminals uh with the first one planned to come online in December already that's uh super fast very impressive how things are moving quickly around here and uh and also uh there are other uh LNG terminals that are being built and expanded in Europe so Germany can also always count on its neighbors for that for example Netherlands it seems to me that LNG is the big bet of Europe to face this crisis in the next couple yes yeah absolutely a big focus and with that in mind the German Chancellor Olaf Schultz is expected to sign LNG contracts during a two-day trip to the Middle East what are the prospects how substantial how significant can those deals be they can be significant and the government has been trying to attack more energy supplies from the middle west for a while now without a lot of success especially with Qatar which is the role one of the world's biggest LNG exporters negotiations has been difficult qatar's strategy is playing a hard ball over prize over duration of contracts so I hope that so the government hopes that they can finally get some negotiations going uh and this duration of energy contracts has been really rich an issue now exporters they want to sign long-term contracts for LNG which usually is more than 20 years so it's a long time it means that Germany would have to be tight with fossil fuels for too long so this is where negotiations get hard and let's okay yeah

videos.title: Germany Nationalizes Uniper to Protect Energy Sector
videos.text: we've been talking then about this potential bailout for for quite a while uh why is it going ahead now what are the details well in many ways this was inevitable uh Juniper we've been told was losing about 100 million euros a day that's after Russia basically shut off the spicket of gas they were the biggest importer of Russian gas and and essentially the German government realized they had to step in they had to step in quickly just underscore how quickly they felt they had to move Chancellor Schultz of course is in New York right now he's at the UN General Assembly and they announced it this morning without the chancellor being in the country feeling they had to take action now to rescue Juniper and make sure that the company stayed afloat Chad what's the reaction been in Germany uh well the German public obviously is very concerned at the same time as this nationalization of juniper which is going to cost billions and billions and we don't know exactly how much will have to be poured in in the coming months so they they're putting in 8 billion today uh to for the nationalization that comes on top of uh much a large amount that they've already given to it but on top of this there's a very controversial gas Levy that's about to be put in place in Germany where people will have to pay more for their energy so people obviously are very angry about that and there's a lot of question whether that Levy is even going to happen
